mnemonics: I-M-P-O-R-T: I/O Methods: Memory-mapped, Port-mapped, Interrupt-driven, DMA, Polling, and their Trade-offs
keyPoints: I/O devices can be accessed via memory-mapped I/O (treated like memory addresses) or port-mapped I/O (using special instructions).
Programmed I/O (polling) is simple but inefficient, while Interrupt-Driven I/O allows the device to signal the CPU when ready.
Direct Memory Access (DMA) allows devices to transfer data directly to/from memory without continuous CPU involvement, greatly improving efficiency.
commonMistakes: Confusing memory-mapped I/O with port-mapped I/O and using the wrong access method (load/store vs. in/out instructions).
Assuming polling is always a bad choice; it can be efficient for simple systems or very fast devices where interrupt overhead is worse.
Forgetting that DMA controllers need to be programmed and managed by the CPU, which adds complexity despite offloading the data transfer task.
